questions.question: What does CDP stand for?
questions.explanation: CDP is the Cisco Discovery Protocol — a Cisco-proprietary L2 discovery protocol; enabled by default on Cisco devices.

questions.question: Which L2 discovery protocol is an industry standard (IEEE 802.1AB)?
questions.explanation: LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol) is the industry-standard L2 discovery protocol (IEEE 802.1AB); disabled by default on Cisco.

questions.question: What is the default CDP message interval?
questions.explanation: The default CDP timer is 60 seconds — this is the CDP message interval.

questions.question: What is the default CDP holdtime?
questions.explanation: The default CDP holdtime is 180 seconds — how long a neighbor is kept after the last BPDU is received.

questions.question: What is the default LLDP holdtime?
questions.explanation: The default LLDP holdtime is 120 seconds (vs. 180 seconds for CDP).

questions.question: Which command globally enables CDP on a Cisco device?
questions.explanation: `cdp run` globally enables CDP. `cdp enable` is per-interface.

questions.question: Which command includes software version, native VLAN, VTP domain, and IP address in the output?
questions.explanation: `show cdp neighbors detail` includes software version, native VLAN, VTP domain, and IP address — beyond the basic neighbor list.

questions.question: What does TLV stand for in CDP/LLDP?
questions.explanation: TLV stands for Type-Length-Value — the format used inside CDP/LLDP advertisements.

questions.question: In CDP capability codes, what does 'R' indicate?
questions.explanation: CDP capability codes: R (router), S (switch), I (IGMP), B (source-route bridge).

questions.question: Which capability is NOT available in LLDP (but is in CDP)?
questions.explanation: LLDP uses R (router), B (bridge) — LLDP has no 'switch' capability.

questions.question: What is the multicast MAC address used by CDP?
questions.explanation: CDP uses multicast MAC 0100.0CCC.CCCC. LLDP uses 0180.C200.000E.

questions.question: What does LLDP separate via 'system capabilities vs enabled capabilities'?
options: What the device can do vs. what is currently active
questions.explanation: LLDP separates what the device *can* do (system capabilities) from what is currently active (enabled capabilities).
